request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"theranos::healthtech","name":"theranos","one_liner":"Theranos claimed to have developed miniaturized, finger-prick blood-testing devices capable of running comprehensive diagnostics from microscopic blood volumes, but the technology was fraudulent and the company collapsed amid criminal fraud convictions.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":2,"rationale":"Both involve a health-data product with a recurring-revenue element (Theranos listed subscription_recurring in trusted facts), but Theranos was fundamentally a B2B/enterprise hardware diagnostics company selling to pharmacy chains, insurers, and clinics. The new pitch is a direct-to-consumer B2C monthly subscription app bundling symptom tracking with telehealth, aimed at individual patients. The business models are structurally very different."},"market":{"score":3,"rationale":"Both operate in the healthtech sector and target patients with health monitoring needs. However, Theranos targeted the US clinical diagnostics market via enterprise partners (Walgreens, insurers, Cleveland Clinic), while the new pitch targets chronic-illness consumers in Germany and France via a digital app. Geography, customer type, regulatory regime, and competitive landscape all diverge materially."},"gtm":{"score":1,"rationale":"Theranos went to market through high-profile enterprise partnerships (Walgreens, Safeway, insurance companies) and relied on celebrity board members and media hype. The new pitch is consumer-direct (app store / digital acquisition) in EU markets. These are near-opposite go-to-market motions."},"stage_scale":{"score":2,"rationale":"Theranos raised $700M+ and reached a $10B peak valuation with hundreds of employees and physical lab infrastructure. The new pitch appears to be an early-stage consumer app. Stage and capital intensity are very different, though both are pre-profitability health ventures targeting underserved patient needs."}},"why_similar":"Both ventures operate in healthtech and are anchored around giving patients more visibility into their own health data, with a recurring-revenue mechanism noted in both. Both face regulated healthcare environments where the accuracy and legitimacy of health information products is scrutinized. The ambition to simplify or democratize patient health management is a common thread.","where_diverged":"The new pitch diverges from Theranos on almost every structural dimension: (1) Geography — Germany and France (EU) vs. US; (2) Customer type — direct-to-consumer chronic-illness patients vs. enterprise pharmacy chains and insurers; (3) Product type — software app with telehealth vs. a physical automated blood-analyzer hardware device; (4) Go-to-market — digital/app-direct vs. B2B partnership with retailers and insurers; (5) Core risk — software product accuracy and regulatory compliance (MDR/digital health) vs. fabricated physical diagnostic hardware claims; (6) Scale — early-stage consumer app vs. a $10B peak-valuation hardware company. The new pitch does not involve any proprietary diagnostic hardware and makes no extraordinary scientific claims, removing Theranos's central fraud vector entirely.","failure_causes":["fraudulent technology claims — core blood-testing device never worked as advertised","systematic concealment of inaccurate test results from regulators, partners, and patients","regulatory rejection — CMS revoked CLIA certification, FDA found multiple violations","enterprise partner collapse — Walgreens and Safeway terminated partnerships after fraud exposure","SEC and criminal fraud charges against founders leading to criminal convictions","insufficient scientific peer review and no published validation data","board dominated by political/military figures with no relevant biomedical or technical expertise"],"lessons_for_input":["Never overstate clinical or diagnostic capabilities — in EU regulated markets (MDR, BfArM, HAS), false efficacy claims trigger swift regulatory action and criminal liability.","Publish or share independent validation data for any health-metric feature; peer review builds trust and is often a regulatory prerequisite in Germany and France.","Design the business model so that revenue does not depend on a single unproven technical claim; for a symptom-tracking and telehealth bundle, ensure each component independently delivers documented patient value.","Engage EU regulatory bodies (BfArM, ANSM) and GDPR data-protection authorities early — chronic-illness health data is among the most sensitive categories under EU law and non-compliance is an existential risk.","Build clinical advisory boards with domain-relevant medical expertise (e.g., chronic disease specialists, licensed telehealth physicians in Germany and France), not just prominent generalist names."]}
